title: The Transatlantic Alliance Can’t Survive Without Trust
gist: Ischinger, chair of the Munich Security Conference, warns that Washington dismisses NATO's value at its own peril as Trump's decade of free-riding accusations culminates in a Hegseth-ordered six-month review of the U.S. military presence in Europe.

themes.theme: U.S. commitment to NATO in doubt

insights.claim: The transatlantic alliance cannot survive without trust, and Washington dismisses NATO's value at its own peril as it questions the U.S. security commitment to Europe.
insights.speaker: Wolfgang Ischinger
insights.so_what: Erosion of allied trust, once lost, is hard to rebuild—a structural risk to European security cohesion and defense planning.

insights.claim: Trump has accused European allies of free-riding on American security guarantees since launching his first campaign in 2015, threatened to pull out of NATO after winning office, and threatened again while running in 2024.
insights.speaker: Wolfgang Ischinger
insights.so_what: A decade-long, consistent pattern signals the NATO-commitment doubt is durable, not a passing negotiating tactic.

insights.claim: At a June 2026 NATO meeting, U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth announced a six-month review of the American military presence in Europe and cast doubt on Washington's payment of its annual NATO dues.
insights.speaker: Wolfgang Ischinger
insights.so_what: A formal force-posture review is a concrete near-term catalyst that could reshape U.S. troop levels in Europe—watch the six-month clock.
insights.locator: Just a few weeks ago, at a NATO meeting
